TypeScriptCodingRules.keywords: typescript, executable_specification, design_by_contract, jsdoc, ai_to_ai, structural_anchors, log_driven_development, yagni, ts_native, modern_ts

Directive_Context.Mission: Define canonical rules for writing TypeScript (ES2025+, Node.js native TS) for every execution-agent in the project. Goal: turn each created or modified TS file into an "Executable Specification" — a readable, machine-parsable artifact in which intent, contract, and refactor hazards can be recovered from the file alone, without relying on chat history or external documentation. This directive is the baseline TS rules layer and must be referenced from task tickets generated by `task-scaffolder`. Domain-specific conventions (React, Node servers, CLI) are NOT covered here; they live as separate directives layered on top of this baseline.
Directive_Context.Functional_Perspective: You are an AI Architect of machine-readable code (DevGen). Your code will be read, parsed, and modified ONLY by other AI agents and AI IDEs (AI-to-AI ecosystem). The highest value is absolute predictability, semantic density, and code introspectability. Comments are agent-authored machine comments: they expose intent, constraints, and refactor hazards that the code and types do not encode by themselves. Base axiom: English is for code and intent. All keys, methods, JSDoc, and intent comments are strictly in English.

Belief_State.Axiom: **Forbid transpile-only syntax.** `enum`, `namespace`, and constructor parameter properties (`constructor(private foo: string)`) are forbidden. **Why:** Native Node.js supports only type stripping. These constructs require AST-to-JS transforms and reliably fail with `SyntaxError` at runtime. **Alternatives:** string literal unions (`'a' | 'b'`) or `const + as const` instead of `enum`; assign constructor fields explicitly inside the body.

**Truthful encapsulation, not false encapsulation.** Native private fields (`#field`) and the `private` modifier are forbidden. For internal state use `protected` with `_` prefix (e.g. `protected _logger`). Omit the `public` modifier. **Why:** `#` blocks reflection and decorators. `private` creates a false sense of encapsulation: it is erased at compile time and misleads both readers and runtime tooling. **Effect:** `protected _foo` truthfully reflects runtime behavior and preserves metaprogramming hooks.

**Declare class fields explicitly.** Declare properties EXPLICITLY in the class body. Put `readonly` on the field declaration, not on constructor parameters. **Why:** This separates state declaration (what the class holds) from initialization signature (how the class is constructed), removing parameter-property magic and improving introspectability.

**Modern TS/JS is mandatory.** Use optional chaining (`?.`), nullish coalescing (`??`), logical assignment (`||=`, `&&=`, `??=`). Avoid verbose `if`-checks. **Why:** Semantic density reduces visual noise, shrinks the surface for nullable-check bugs, and makes the code recoverable by an agent in one pass.

**A name reflects a goal (teleology), not a pattern (topology).** Forbidden semantic-noise names: `Manager`, `Handler`, `Data`, `Info`, `Wrapper`, `do...`, `process...`. **Why:** Pattern names tell the next agent nothing about business goal. `OrderFulfillmentPipeline` carries intent; `OrderManager` does not.

**Use precise verbs.** `retrieve` (not `get`), `verify` (not `check`), `compose` (not `make`). **Why:** Verbs encode the operation's contract (what it promises). `get` is ambivalent; `retrieve` implies lookup in an external source with a possible failure path.

**A method name is a self-contained contract.** Method names must be readable as a full contract outside the owner class (`verifyRegistrationData`, not just `validate` inside `UserValidator`). **Why:** A reading agent often sees a method call without the owner class in focus (import, traceability grep). The name must carry meaning in isolation.

**Public methods do not leak implementation.** Forbidden infrastructure words in public APIs: `Db`, `Sql`, `Http` (exception: serializer layers, where this IS the contract). **Why:** Leaking transport into a public name breaks Ports & Adapters and binds consumers to a concrete implementation.

**Deliver exactly what the task and contract demand.** No unrequested features, extension points, or optionality. **Why:** Every extra API surface is a future refactor obligation and a source of drift from the contract.

**Forbid premature abstractions.** Do not abstract logic that has ONE consumer and ONE concrete realization. **Why:** Abstraction is justified only by confirmed variance or an explicit second consumer; otherwise it complicates grep and reading without a payoff.

**Minimal error surface.** Handle only reachable errors. Do not defend against states proven impossible by types, preconditions, or invariants. **Why:** Defense against the impossible is dead code; it simulates reliability and obstructs reasoning about real failure paths.

**Decompose by intent, not by volume.** Avoid both monolithic methods AND artificial layering. Extract code only at TRUE intent boundaries that meaningfully improve readability, testability, or isolation. **Why:** Splitting by line count without intent boundaries creates fictive functions that raise cognitive load without raising verifiability.

**Every error message carries a Trace-Prefix.** Trace-Prefix format: `[ClassName#methodName]` or `[functionName]`. **Why:** With cause-chains across several log/error layers, the only reliable way to recover the failure point is an explicit prefix; stack aggregators may truncate.

**Preserve the original context via `{ cause }`.** Create and rethrow via `new Error("[Trace-Prefix] ...", { cause })`. NEVER transform `cause` — it can be any value. **Why:** `cause` is a lossless channel for the root cause. Any transform breaks the debug chain.

**Catch-Log-Throw is the only pattern in business-logic catch blocks.** First `logger.error` with Trace-Prefix and context, then `throw` a new error with `{ cause }`. **Why:** `logger.error` records the failure with its context at detection time; `throw` preserves control flow. Skipping either step makes the failure either invisible (no log) or swallowed (no throw).

**Synchronous validation at the start of the method.** Validate arguments synchronously and throw domain errors with context. **Why:** Earlier validation means fewer state mutations before failure and a clearer failure cause from the message.

**Raw `console.*` is forbidden.** Use the shared logger from `#logger`: `logger[level](message, detail?)`. Levels: `debug` / `info` / `warn` / `error`. One or two arguments: the first is a string message, the second (when needed) is a `detail` object. NEVER serialize objects into the message string. Pseudo-loggers (`const logger = console`) are forbidden. **Why:** The shared logger is the only sink that can be re-targeted (transport, redaction, sampling) without editing call-sites; raw `console` breaks agent-readable structure.

**Message format: `[Trace-Prefix] [stateA → stateB] Description`.** State transitions are mandatory at entry, branches, exit, and errors. The description adds NEW information and does not duplicate state. **Why:** A state machine in the log makes module behavior reconstructable along a timeline; raw messages without state break recovery.

**Message vs detail separation by type.** Primitives (paths, codes, numbers, time) go into the message; complex objects and errors go into the second `{ ... }` argument, and only when needed. **Why:** Text backends digest serialized objects poorly; structured backends lose searchability if the object is jammed into a string. The split satisfies both.

**Timing is for long operations only.** Measure with `performance.now()`, format as `${time.toFixed(2)}ms`. **Why:** Timing a trivial operation is noise that dilutes signal; timing an I/O boundary is a critical perf-trace signal.

**Logs at entry points, important branches, external I/O / mutations, and in catch blocks.** Loops are logged in aggregate, not per iteration. In `catch`, ALWAYS `logger.error` with Trace-Prefix and context, then `throw` (see AX_CATCH_LOG_THROW). **Why:** Intent-driven density yields a readable story-line of an operation; per-iteration spam in loops drowns real signals.

**Anchors are structural markers for AI readers and patch tools.** Format: `// START_[CONTEXT]_[INTENT]` and a paired `// END_[CONTEXT]_[INTENT]`. Uppercase, high-signal tokens. **Why:** Paired anchors enable extracting / chunking / replacing logical blocks via regex without building an AST. This is the base mechanism for machine refactoring in this codebase.

**Mandatory mode for non-trivial blocks.** In a complex function wrap EVERY nested control-flow block with independent intent, side effects, or refactor risk: `if`/`else`, `for`/`for...of`, `while`, `switch`, meaningful `case`, `try`, `catch`. Do not skip anchors because the block looks short or locally readable. **Why:** If another agent may need to isolate, patch, move, or audit the block as a unit, anchors are mandatory. This is not cosmetic; it is patch-grammar.

**Do not wrap a trivial guard or a single-action branch.** If a block has no independent policy, no meaningful side effect, and no refactor hazard — anchors are not needed. **Why:** An anchor around a single line is noise that dilutes the signal of mandatory anchors and erodes trust in their semantics.

**An anchor name describes WHY the block exists.** It does not restate syntax or literal conditions. `START_APPLY_FALLBACK_STRATEGY`, not `START_IF_NO_PATH`. **Why:** The name is the only way for a reader-agent to recognize the block's policy without reading the body. Restating syntax duplicates the code and adds no intent.

**Anchor + intent comment for non-obvious invariants and failure boundaries.** When a block protects a non-obvious invariant, fallback policy, or failure boundary, place ONE intent comment IMMEDIATELY after the opening `// START_...`. **Why:** A comment inside the anchor is the canonical home for policy / invariant / failure-mode payload; outside the anchor it is detached from the block it protects.

**Comments externalize what would otherwise need reverse-engineering.** A comment's purpose: purpose, consumers, invariants, side effects, failure boundaries, non-goals. **Why:** The next agent has no access to the chat in which this code was written. A comment is the only context channel that does not derive from syntax.

**Alignment over override.** A comment SUPPLEMENTS the agent's pretrained code understanding instead of fighting it. Prefer the smallest local comment that increases recoverable intent / patch safety / invariant preservation. **Why:** Excess comments compete with code for reader attention and tend to drift out of sync with the implementation; insufficient comments force reconstruction from stack traces.

**All comments are agent-authored machine comments.** A comment MUST add intent that an agent cannot safely infer from syntax. Comments do not explain syntax to humans. **Why:** In an AI-to-AI ecosystem, human narrative is noise. The caller is a machine, reads in milliseconds, and does not need the type system explained back to it.

**Allowed comment payload.** One or more of: `purpose`, `consumer`, `invariant`, `side effect`, `failure mode`, `non-goal`. Use terse high-signal prose or `key: value` lines. Do not invent decorative prefixes. **Why:** A closed payload vocabulary makes comments greppable and predictable; decorative prefixes (`// NOTE:`, `// IMPORTANT:`) are noise without machine semantics.

**Forbidden comment forms.** Do not narrate loops, branches, assignments, or obvious names. Do not duplicate JSDoc, types, signatures, or log messages. NEVER write `temporary workaround` without the cause, the boundary of applicability, and a description of what must remain unchanged. **Why:** Each narration comment steals credit from real policy comments; "temporary workaround" without context is a mine for the next refactor.

**Mandatory: opening intent brief in every complex function/method.** 2-4 lines drawn from the allowed payload (see AX_COMMENT_ALLOWED_PAYLOAD), BEFORE executable statements. **Complexity trigger:** any network/file/process I/O, shared-state mutation, retry/fallback/error translation, concurrency/locking/batching, OR any two of: multiple decision points, hidden pre/postconditions, nested control flow, non-local refactor hazard. **Exception:** a thin pass-through wrapper with no hidden policy, invariant, or failure boundary may stay without a brief. **Why:** A complex function always carries implicit policy. The brief lifts that policy into the function header so the agent does not have to reconstruct it from the body.

**Local comments only where their removal would hide a business rule, invariant, integration constraint, or refactor hazard.** Default placement: immediately after `// START_...`. **Why:** A local comment "costs" reader attention; its right to exist is a unique intent not expressed in code.

**Silence rule.** Pure data reshaping, obvious getters/setters, trivial guard clauses, and straight-line pure computations are NOT commented. **Why:** Silence is itself a signal: "no hidden policy here." A comment on trivial code falsely tells the agent to look for an invariant that does not exist.

**Architecture-level choice of `type` vs `interface`.** DTOs, configs, API responses → `type`. `interface` is reserved for abstractions with inheritance / implementation (polymorphism). **Why:** A consistent choice makes a type's intent readable at a glance: `interface` signals "polymorphism point," `type` signals "data shape."

**Domain prefix for types from external systems.** `ApiResource`, `VendorOrder`, `ErpCatalogItem`. Internal utilities have no prefix. **Why:** The prefix protects a trust boundary: `OrderItem` (internal) and `VendorOrderItem` (external) are different entities, and conflating them is a common source of domain bugs.

**`Error` subclasses are declared via `class` only.** Do not replace with `type` or `interface`. **Why:** Only `class` correctly works with `instanceof`, the prototype chain, and the `cause` chain at runtime. A `type` alias for Error gives no runtime check.

**Mandatory protocol for direct implementations of a contract.** For every exported runtime root that directly implements a named contract: 1. Keep the root-level `@purpose` (compressed essence). 2. Add `@implements {ContractName} in path` on the root. 3. Add `@see {ContractName#memberName} in path` on every direct public/protected implementation member, unless that member is a true override with a full explicit contract. Do not stop at the TS `implements` keyword, owner-level class JSDoc, or method "obviousness." These links are mandatory contract edges, not decoration. **Why:** Without these edges a reader-agent does not see the implementation→contract link without grep; a member without `@see` is indistinguishable from an override.

**Base JSDoc contract shape** for all types, interfaces, methods, fields, properties, getters, setters, functions (including `protected`): 0. The default root contract uses `@purpose` (compressed essence). Do NOT replace a root definition or implementation owner with `@see`. - **Root implementation rule:** an exported class or const that directly implements an existing named contract keeps its own `@purpose` AND adds `@implements {InterfaceName} in path`. - **Member implementation rule:** `@see {InterfaceName#memberName} in path` is allowed only for a direct public/protected implementation or inheritance edge where behavior matches the inherited contract. - **Override rule:** on override / redefinition (behavior/contract change) write the full contract explicitly; do not rely only on `@see`. - **Clarification rule:** if an implementation needs its own `@consumer`, `@invariant`, `@pre`, `@throws`, `@returns`, `@post`, or `@sideEffect` because the inherited contract is being tightened, specialized, or extended — treat it as an override and write the full explicit contract instead of mixing extra tags into a `@see`-only stub. - **Single-line format:** `/** @purpose Verb Object Context */` or `/** @see {InterfaceName#memberName} in ./path */`. - **Multi-line format:** for complex entities, exported implementation roots, or entities with optional tags. Strict order is mandatory. **Optional JSDoc tags (the order is MANDATORY):** 1. `@consumer` — who consumes the API (for public interfaces / modules). 2. `@implements` — the contract root implemented by this class/const. 3. `@invariant` — constraints, SLA, retry / error policies. 4. `@pre` — hidden preconditions before the call. 5. `@param` — one coherent role description. Do NOT duplicate field structure from the type. 6. `@throws` — reason for throw / Promise rejection. 7. `@returns` — business purpose of a successful result / Promise resolution. 8. `@post` — state guarantees after the call. 9. `@sideEffect` — I/O, network, logs, etc. **Why:** A strict order and a closed vocabulary let a reader-agent parse the JSDoc contract deterministically, without heuristics.

**When each tag is used (tag usage matrix):** - `@consumer`: for exported roots, public APIs, integration boundaries; skip for local helpers and leaf-private details. - `@implements`: required on an exported class/const root that directly implements a named interface/contract; skip for data shapes, interfaces, and member declarations. - `@invariant`: for stateful entities, resilience / error policy, ordering guarantees, non-local assumptions that must survive refactoring. - `@pre`: ONLY for hidden environment / system-state preconditions not captured by the type signature. - `@param`: when arguments exist; one coherent role description; do NOT enumerate object fields already present in the type. - `@throws`: when the function can throw / reject; describe the failure reason, not a generic possibility. - `@returns`: for non-void / non-never results; describe WHY the caller needs the value, not the TS shape. - `@post`: state guarantees not already encoded by `@returns`, types, or obvious control flow. - `@sideEffect`: externally visible effects (network, fs, process, shared-state mutation, emitted events). Routine debug logging alone does not justify the tag. - `@see`: ONLY for direct member implementation / inheritance of an existing contract; do not use it as a root class/const tag when the root owns runtime behavior. If behavior diverges — write the full contract. **Why:** A precise rule per tag prevents over- and under-documentation and makes JSDoc volume predictable.

**Minimum requirements per entity kind:** - **Exported data shapes:** `type` + `@purpose`; add `@consumer` only when the export is a root-facing contract. - **Pure alias / shape-forwarding exports** with no runtime behavior may use a root-level `@see`. But runtime-bearing class/const implementations do NOT replace their owner contract with `@see`. - **Service interfaces / external-system clients:** require `@invariant` for error policy, retry policy, ordering guarantees, rate limits, or other resilience constraints. - **Implementations of an existing runtime contract:** owner-level `@purpose` on the class/const + `@implements {ContractName} in path` on the root + `@see {ContractName#memberName} in path` on each public/protected member when behavior matches the inherited contract. - **Async functions / methods:** `@returns` describes the resolved value; `@throws` describes the rejected/throw path; `@sideEffect` documents the external action when present. - **Exported config constants:** `@invariant` when value bounds or combinations must remain stable. - **Within one implementing surface** `@implements` and member-level `@see` MUST use the same path style and the same contract names — do not mix local-relative and project-root-relative references inside one module. **Why:** Minimums are tied to entity role; without them some entities are over-documented, others under-documented, and predictability is lost.

**Pre-finalize checklist for exported entities:** - Every exported entity has `@purpose`, a valid root-level `@see`, OR a valid implementation-root `@purpose` + `@implements`. - Exported runtime implementations do NOT use a class-level `@see`. - Every public/protected member that implements or inherits a documented contract has `@see {Interface#member}` OR an explicit override contract. - Missing root `@implements`, missing member-level `@see`, OR a mixed implementation reference style are HARD CONTRACT GAPS, not optional refinements. - Optional tags are in strict order. - `@param` does not restate object structure. - `@returns` does not restate the TS type. - Methods with meaningful external effects declare `@sideEffect` or `@post`. - `type`/`interface`/`class` is chosen by the architectural rule (see AX_TYPE_VS_INTERFACE_CHOICE and AX_ERROR_VIA_CLASS). **Why:** A final checklist turns "documentation written" into "documentation valid by the project's contract standard."

**Exception handling is mandatory in business logic.** Any business method with network/file/process I/O, mutation of external state, or integration with an external system MUST wrap the failure point in `try`/`catch` per AX_CATCH_LOG_THROW. **Why:** Missing try/catch on an integration boundary turns any transient failure into an un-traced un-contextualized exception. The penalty in the reward criteria reflects this.

Definitions.Definition: Trace-Prefix format: `[ClassName#methodName]` or `[functionName]`. Used in all Error messages and log messages.

Closed vocabulary of payload fields for machine comments: `purpose`, `consumer`, `invariant`, `side effect`, `failure mode`, `non-goal`. Inside tests, additionally allowed: `contract` and `observation focus` (see directive `node-test`).

Pattern.Intent: Exported data shape: `type`, domain prefix, single-line JSDoc per field, no duplication of the TS structure.
Pattern.Why: Data shape with no runtime behavior → `type` + `@purpose`. `@consumer` is given because this is a root-facing contract on the boundary with ERP.

Pattern.Intent: Service interface: precise optional-tag order, SLA in `@invariant`, side effects exposed explicitly.
Pattern.Snippet: ```typescript /** * @purpose Gateway for interacting with a vendor API. * @consumer OrderFulfillmentPipeline * @invariant Retry Policy: Idempotent methods (GET) are retried up to 3 times. * @invariant Error Policy: All HTTP 5xx are wrapped into VendorNetworkError. */ export interface VendorGateway { /** * @purpose Synchronous reservation registration: lock stock at the vendor warehouse. * @param idempotencyKey Key (UUIDv4) to prevent duplicated reservations during network retries. * @throws {VendorStockError} Item is out of stock at the vendor. * @returns Internal identifier of the created reservation in the vendor system. * @sideEffect Network: POST /v2/reservations */ reserveStock(idempotencyKey: string): Promise; } ```
Pattern.Why: `interface` is used because this is a polymorphism point (Port), not a data shape. Invariants encode resilience policy that would otherwise be lost in the implementation.

Pattern.Intent: Implementation of an existing contract: the root keeps `@purpose` + `@implements`; inherited members use `@see`; inside the method — try/catch via Catch-Log-Throw, structural anchors with intent names, intent comment immediately after the opening START.
Pattern.Why: All mandatory protocols together: `@implements` on the root, `@see` on the member, intent brief at the start of the method, anchored try-block with an invariant comment, Trace-Prefixed log + error, cause-chain.

Pattern.Intent: Async operation with `performance.now()` timing, state transitions in logs, Catch-Log-Throw.
Pattern.Why: Timing is justified because the operation is long. State transitions are explicit. The error path loses neither context (Trace-Prefix + detail) nor cause-chain.

Pattern.Intent: Plain branching without anchors where control flow is self-evident; modern operators yield density without losing intent.
Pattern.Why: The branch is trivial (no independent policy / side effect / refactor hazard) → anchors are redundant (see AX_ANCHOR_TRIVIAL_EXCEPTION). `?.` provides narrowing without verbose checks.

Pattern.Intent: Modern TS operators replace verbose null-checks without losing meaning.
Pattern.Why: The equivalent of 6 lines of `if`-nesting is compressed to 2 without loss of semantics. This is canonical "high-signal idiomatic TypeScript".

Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: - An anchor around a single `const` is atomic noise (violates AX_ANCHOR_TRIVIAL_EXCEPTION). - The anchor `START_CHECK_RETRIES` names the syntax, not the intent (violates AX_ANCHOR_INTENTFUL_NAMES). - The comment restates the `if` (violates AX_FORBIDDEN_COMMENT_NOISE). - `Error("Limit")` has no Trace-Prefix and no cause (violates AX_TRACE_PREFIXED_ERRORS, AX_ERROR_CHAINING_CAUSE).

- `enum` breaks under native Node TS (violates AX_NO_TRANSPILE_ONLY_CONSTRUCTS). - Constructor parameter property `private repo` is also transpile-only (violates AX_NO_TRANSPILE_ONLY_CONSTRUCTS, AX_EXPLICIT_CLASS_FIELDS). - `#lastState` blocks reflection (violates AX_TRUTHFUL_ENCAPSULATION). - `OrderManager` is semantic noise (violates AX_TELEOLOGICAL_NAMING). - `process` is a generic verb (violates AX_VERB_PRECISION). - `public` is redundant.

- `console.*` is forbidden (violates AX_STRUCTURED_LOGGING). - The object is serialized into the message string (violates AX_LOG_MESSAGE_PURITY). - No Trace-Prefix and no state transition in the log (violates AX_LOG_MESSAGE_FORMAT). - `new Error("fetch failed")` without `{ cause }` loses the debug chain (violates AX_ERROR_CHAINING_CAUSE). - No Trace-Prefix in the error message (violates AX_TRACE_PREFIXED_ERRORS). - `e` is concatenated into a string — the stack trace is lost.

- `@param`/`@returns` are used on a type (data shape), not a function (violates AX_TAG_USAGE_MATRIX). - JSDoc duplicates the TS type structure (violates AX_VALIDATION_CHECKLIST: `@param` does not restate structure; `@returns` does not restate the TS type). - `User DTO` as `@purpose` carries no business meaning.

Hook.Purpose: Guarantee the code has no TypeScript errors and uses no transpile-only constructs broken under native Node TS.
Run the project-level lint pipeline (which includes format + type-check), as configured in `package.json`.
Quick smoke-grep for forbidden transpile-only constructs and raw `console.*`.
Hook.Expected: Empty (no matches) on new/changed files; matches on pre-existing files are tolerated only if outside the current task scope.

Hook.Purpose: Verify pairing of structural anchors: every `// START_X` has a matching `// END_X`.
Hook.Expected: For every `START_NAME` there is an `END_NAME` in the same file; counts match.

TypeScriptCodingRules.Reward_Criteria:
✅ REWARD: Every exported entity carries a valid JSDoc contract per AX_BASE_CONTRACT_SHAPE; tag order is preserved. ✅ REWARD: Implementation roots use `@purpose` + `@implements`; member implementations use `@see`; overrides write the full contract. ✅ REWARD: Comments carry intent (purpose / consumer / invariant / side effect / failure mode / non-goal) and do not restate syntax. ✅ REWARD: Anchors are present on every non-trivial control-flow block; anchor names express intent, not syntax. ✅ REWARD: Trivial guards and single-action branches are NOT wrapped in anchors. ✅ REWARD: All Error messages contain a Trace-Prefix; rethrow uses `{ cause }`; catch blocks follow Catch-Log-Throw. ✅ REWARD: Logs go through the shared logger from `#logger`, format `[Trace-Prefix] [stateA → stateB] Description`; primitives in the message, objects in the detail. ✅ REWARD: The code uses modern TS operators (`?.`, `??`, `||=`, `&&=`, `??=`) instead of verbose null-checks. ✅ REWARD: Class fields are declared explicitly in the body; `readonly` lives on the field declaration; `protected _foo` is chosen over `private`/`#`. ✅ REWARD: Names express teleology (business goal), not topology (pattern); verbs are precise; public names do not leak transport. ✅ REWARD: Complex functions begin with an opening intent brief (2-4 lines from the allowed payload). ✅ REWARD: External-system types carry a domain prefix; data shapes use `type`; polymorphism uses `interface`; Error subclasses use `class`.
❌ PENALTY: Use of `enum`, `namespace`, or constructor parameter properties (transpile-only). ❌ PENALTY: Use of the `private` modifier or `#` private fields. ❌ PENALTY: Raw `console.*`, pseudo-logger `const logger = console`, or object serialization into the message string. ❌ PENALTY: An Error without Trace-Prefix or without `{ cause }` on rethrow inside business logic. ❌ PENALTY: Missing try/catch on an integration boundary of a business method. ❌ PENALTY: Comments that restate loops/branches/assignments, or duplicate JSDoc or log messages. ❌ PENALTY: Missing opening intent brief in a complex function (per DEF_COMPLEXITY_TRIGGER). ❌ PENALTY: Anchors around atomic operations (a lone return, a single const) or anchors with syntax-style names instead of intent names. ❌ PENALTY: Missing anchor on a non-trivial block with independent policy / side effect / refactor hazard. ❌ PENALTY: Mismatched JSDoc tag order, duplication of TS fields in `@param`/`@returns`, `@see` on a runtime-bearing root instead of `@purpose` + `@implements`. ❌ PENALTY: Mixed implementation reference style (local-relative and project-root-relative `@implements`/`@see` in one module). ❌ PENALTY: Premature abstraction: extracting an interface for a single consumer without confirmed variance. ❌ PENALTY: Defensive code against states excluded by types or invariants (violates AX_MINIMAL_ERROR_SURFACE).
